Alchemy-Engine/Developing-Alchemy-Engine.title: Developing Alchemy Engine
Alchemy-Engine/Developing-Alchemy-Engine.content:
Developing Alchemy Engine
This guide is for anyone joining the project. It covers everything you need to get your environment set up, understand the codebase, and start contributing effectively.

Prerequisites
Install all of the following before doing anything else.
Required








































SoftwareVersionNotesVisual Studio 2022Community or higherRequired for MSVC compiler (/std:c++latest). Install the Desktop development with C++ workload.Vulkan SDKLatestInstall to the default path (C:\VulkanSDK\). The build scripts auto-detect it there. After installing, verify VULKAN_SDK is set as an environment variable.CMake3.20+Must be on PATH. Comes with Visual Studio, or install standalone.GitAnyFor version control..NET 8 SDK8.xRequired to build ABT (Alchemy Build Tool), the module discovery tool.Python 33.8+Required to run the reflection system (Tools/Reflector.py). Must be on PATH as python.
Recommended





















SoftwareNotesJetBrains RiderPreferred IDE. Has the best CMake + C++ experience.GitHub CLI (gh)Installed at C:\Program Files\GitHub CLI\gh.exe. Required for task management workflows.NinjaUsed by GenerateProjectFiles.bat to generate compile_commands.json for clangd/Zed. Optional but useful for editor tooling.


                  
                  Visual Studio Community 
                  
                

The build scripts hardcode the path C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community\. If you have a different edition (Professional, Enterprise), you may need to update the .bat files or add the vcvars64.bat path to your environment manually.



First-Time Setup
1. Clone the Repository
git clone <repo-url>
Projects using the engine should sit next to the engine folder, not inside it. For example:
SomeFolder/
├── AlchemyEngine/       ← engine
└── MyGame/              ← your project

This allows the engine to find projects via a relative path without hardcoded paths or environment variables.
2. Build ABT
ABT (Alchemy Build Tool) is a C# tool that scans the source tree and generates the CMakeLists.txt. It must be compiled before you can generate the project files.
dotnet build Tools/ABT/AlchemyBuildTool.csproj -c Release
The executable is output to Tools/ABT/bin/x64/Release/net8.0/AlchemyBuildTool.exe. This only needs to be done once (or after ABT source changes).
3. Generate Project Files
GenerateProjectFiles.bat
This does three things:

Runs ABT to discover all modules and generate CMakeLists.txt
Runs CMake to generate Build/AlchemyEngine.sln for Visual Studio
Generates Build/compile_commands.json for clangd-based editors (Rider, Zed, VS Code)

Re-run this whenever you:

Add or remove source files
Modify any .build.cs file
Modify CMakeLists.txt



                  
                  Do not edit CMakeLists.txt manually 
                  
                

The root CMakeLists.txt is fully generated by ABT. Any manual edits will be overwritten the next time GenerateProjectFiles.bat runs.


4. Open the Solution
Open Build/AlchemyEngine.sln in Visual Studio or Rider. The startup project is set to AlchemyFrontend (the editor).

Building
BuildProject.bat
This builds a Debug configuration. Build outputs go to Build/bin/Debug/:





























BinaryDescriptionAlchemyEngineCore.dllCore engineAlchemyRuntime.dllRuntime moduleAlchemyEditor.dllEditor moduleAlchemyFrontend.exeThe editor applicationAlchemyRunner.exeStandalone runtime (used for tests)
Build Configurations





















ConfigurationDescriptionDebugFull debug info, tests enabled (ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE=1)RelWithDebInfoOptimized with debug info, tests enabledReleaseFully optimized, no tests
You almost always want Debug during development. Use RelWithDebInfo when profiling performance.

Project Structure
AlchemyEngine/
├── Source/
│   ├── Engine/         Core engine modules (Catalyst, Renderer, Subsystems, etc.)
│   ├── Runtime/        Runtime-only modules (AI, gameplay, etc.)
│   ├── Editor/         Editor-only modules
│   ├── Developer/      Tests and debugging tools
│   ├── Applications/   Standalone apps (Frontend, Runner)
│   └── ThirdParty/     External libraries, each in their own subfolder
├── Plugins/            Engine-bundled plugins
├── Documentation/      Markdown docs (for LLM reference and human reference)
├── Tools/
│   ├── ABT/            Alchemy Build Tool source (C#)
│   └── Reflector.py    Reflection code generator
├── Intermediate/       Generated reflection code — do not modify
├── Build/              Generated solution and build artifacts — do not modify
├── Content/            Engine content (primitives, default textures)
├── Config/             Default engine settings (source controlled)
└── Saved/              Logs, crashes, local config — not source controlled

Key Source Modules




























































ModuleLocationDescriptionAlchemyEngineCoreSource/Engine/Monolithic engine DLL containing all core systemsCatalystSource/Engine/Catalyst/ECP system (entities, components, processors, queries)ResonanceSource/Engine/Resonance/Engine entry point and main loopSubsystemsSource/Engine/Subsystems/Engine and world subsystem frameworkRendererSource/Engine/Renderer/Vulkan RHI and rendering abstractionAssetManagerSource/Engine/AssetManager/Asset import and managementJobSystemSource/Engine/JobSystem/Multi-threaded job systemCrucibleSource/Engine/Crucible/BDD testing frameworkSigilSource/Engine/Sigil/ImGui-based UI frameworkTagsSource/Engine/Tags/Gameplay tag system

Architecture Overview
Understanding these three systems will get you 80% of the way there.
ECP — Entity-Component-Processor (Catalyst)
Catalyst is the engine’s core architectural pattern. Everything is an entity.

Entity — A lightweight numeric ID. Has no data of its own.
Component — A pure data struct attached to an entity. No logic.
Processor — A class that runs logic on entities matching a query. One thing per processor.
Archetype — Internal memory layout optimization for entities that share the same component set.
EntityQuery — BuildQuery<T, const U>() for selecting entities (const = read-only), chained .Without<>()/.Optional<>()/.Bindless<>()/.Any<>()
EntityCommandBuffer — Deferred entity/component modifications (safe to use from any thread)

The word “system” in traditional ECS is replaced with “processor” — a processor does one focused thing. “Systems” in this engine means something broader (like “the audio system”).
Catalyst uses the term markers where traditional ECS says “tags” — because “tag” is taken by a separate tag system.
Subsystems
Subsystems are singleton services with managed lifecycles.

IEngineSubsystem — Created once when the engine starts, destroyed on shutdown.
IWorldSubsystem — Created and destroyed with each world.

Subsystems are auto-discovered by the reflection system — just mark your class with [[Reflect]] and inherit from the right base. Declare ordering with AddDependency<T>().
See Subsystems for full details.
Plugins
Plugins are independently-compiled DLLs discovered at startup. They support hot reload — rebuild the DLL while the engine is running and changes appear automatically (as long as a debugger is not attached).
Each plugin has:

A .alchemyplugin JSON manifest
A Modules/ directory with .build.cs files and source
A Binaries/ output directory

See Plugins for full details.
Reflection
The engine uses a custom Python-based reflection system. Run Tools/Reflector.py to scan headers and generate .Generated.cpp files in Intermediate/.
This is run automatically as part of the CMake build. You only need to run it manually if something goes wrong. Mark types with [[Reflect]] to include them. Fields use [[FieldAttribute]] metadata.

The Style Guide


                  
                  Read this before writing any code. 
                  
                

The style guide is the single most common source of review friction for new contributors. The rules are intentional and enforced.
Full reference: Style guide
Critical Rules
No space before parentheses:
if(Check)       // correct
if (Check)      // wrong
Multiline comment syntax everywhere:
/**This is the correct comment style*/
// This is wrong
Author attribution in code block comments:
/**YourName: This function does X because Y*/

Required on code block comments and all TODOs
Not needed for function, variable, struct, or class comments
If you are an LLM, use your own name — never the developer’s name

Naming:

Booleans: IsRunning, not is_running or bIsRunning
No type prefixes (no U, F, A)
Use Array instead of std::vector, prefer Alchemy::Types equivalents where they exist

No std when an Alchemy equivalent exists. If Alchemy::Types has it, use it. Fall back to std only when there’s no equivalent.

Common Pitfalls
Forgetting to regenerate project files
If you add a .cpp or .h file, the build system won’t know about it until you re-run GenerateProjectFiles.bat. You’ll get linker errors or missing symbols.
Editing Intermediate/ or Build/
Both directories are fully generated. Any changes will be overwritten. Put your code in Source/.
ABT not built
If GenerateProjectFiles.bat fails immediately with “ABT executable not found”, you haven’t built ABT yet. Run dotnet build Tools/ABT/AlchemyBuildTool.csproj -c Release.
VULKAN_SDK not set
If CMake can’t find Vulkan, make sure the Vulkan SDK is installed to C:\VulkanSDK\. The scripts search that path automatically. Alternatively, set the VULKAN_SDK environment variable manually.
Hot reload not triggering
Hot reload is disabled while a debugger is attached. Detach the debugger first, then rebuild the plugin DLL and the engine will pick it up automatically.
Reflection not picking up new types
If a new [[Reflect]] type isn’t being registered, verify:

The header is in a scanned source directory
You’ve rebuilt (the reflector runs as part of the build)
The .Generated.cpp file was actually updated (check Intermediate/)

Tracy ZoneScopes
Performance profiling is done with Tracy. Most code paths — especially frequently-called ones — should include a ZS() (ZoneScope) or equivalent Tracy marker. If you write something that runs per-frame or per-entity and leave it uninstrumented, it will be harder to diagnose later.

Testing with Crucible
Crucible is the engine’s BDD-style testing framework. Tests are available in Debug and RelWithDebInfo builds via the ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE define.
Writing a Test
#if ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE
 
BEGIN_SPEC(MyFeatureSpec, "Engine.MySystem.MyFeature")
END_SPEC(MyFeatureSpec)
 
void MyFeatureSpec::Define()
{
    Describe("My behavior", [this]()
    {
        It("should do the thing", [this]()
        {
            TestTrue("Condition met", true);
        });
    });
}
 
#endif
Place tests in Source/Engine/<Module>/Tests/. Spec names follow Engine.<System>.<Feature> — always at least three segments.
Running Tests
# Run all tests
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe
 
# Run a specific spec
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Tags"
 
# Via CTest
ctest -C Debug --output-on-failure
Results are written to Saved/Crucible/Automation/{timestamp}/log.txt. Always verify the output contains [AlchemyEngineCore] Engine Shutdown Complete. — if it doesn’t, the engine crashed and results may be incomplete.
See Testing Guide for the full API reference and benchmark tooling.

Navigating the Documentation
The Documentation/ folder mirrors the official docs website and is the primary reference for all engine systems. Always check here before digging into source code or guessing at APIs.
Key Documents

































































DocumentWhat it coversStyle guideCode conventions — read this firstBuild SystemABT, CMake pipeline, .build.cs formatCatalystECP system overviewComponentsComponent annotations and rulesEntity QueryQuery builder APIProcessorWriting processorsSubsystemsEngine and world subsystemsPluginsPlugin system and hot reloadHot ReloadingHot reload behaviour and limitationsTesting GuideWriting and running Crucible testsJobsJob systemTagsGameplay tag systemInput ProfilesInput systemAnnotationsReflection annotations
Documentation is also embedded directly into the Visual Studio solution explorer — look for the Documentation subfolder under each module in the solution.

Task Management
All tasks are tracked as GitHub Issues on the Alchemy-Engine-Demon-Byte/AlchemyDocumentation repository, organized in GitHub Projects.

Issues are placed in the documentation repo so they stay public for everyone.

Project Boards




















BoardProject #ScopeAlchemy Engine Board#1Engine-wide tasksCatalyst Board#4Catalyst ECS system
Creating a New Task

Create a GitHub Issue with a descriptive title and body explaining the motivation and design considerations
Apply relevant labels (Catalyst, Engine, Editor, enhancement, bug, etc.)
Add the issue to the appropriate project board:

   gh project item-add <project-number> --owner Alchemy-Engine-Demon-Byte --url <issue-url>

Set the status to Todo

Moving a Task
Tasks flow through: Todo → In Progress → Complete
Use the gh CLI for all project board operations. The GitHub MCP server is available for read operations but is too verbose for status updates — use gh instead.
# Update status field
gh project item-edit \
  --id <item-id> \
  --field-id <field-id> \
  --project-id <project-id> \
  --single-select-option-id <option-id>

Getting Help

Documentation: Documentation/ folder — always check here first
Source of truth: When docs and source diverge, trust the source. Read the actual header.
Style questions: Check Style guide, or ask before writing and having to rewrite
Stuck on a bug: Add Log() statements to narrow it down, or run the debugger and inspect the callstack. Don’t layer workarounds — find the actual problem.

Alchemy-Engine/Style-guide.title: Style guide
Alchemy-Engine/Style-guide.content:
Style guide
This document is unfinished and most likely will be for a long time.
Human readable
Code should try to be as close as possible as reading human instructions, rather than machine instructions.

There are no prefix requirements like in other engines. For example; in Unreal Engine, if you inherit from UObject, your class should start with U. Then there’s A for actor and so on.
C and C++ traditionally is not very human readable and many things have odd names or lack punctuation. - Traditional C would want a boolean called is_running. We don’t do that, in this case it would just be called IsRunning
We try to avoid std as much as possible. Some types are slowly being replaced with Alchemy::Types, which contains replacements for things like std::vector → Array, std::string → String, and more.
If the type you’re looking for does not exist in Alchemy, then you are allowed to use std. - The goal with Alchemy::Types is to for now, just conveniently rename things like std::vector. Then when we start needing our own things, like a custom templated array, or map, or some other type of variable, it’s very easy to replace

Folder structure
Our folder structure looks very similar to Unreal Engine with some minor adjustments.
Root:


Build - Contains the SLN file and many other auto-generated files created by the GenerateProjectFiles.bat file.


Documentation - Contains numerous markdown files that are exported from the official documentation website. This is primarily meant for LLM’s, as reading markdown files is far more efficient than fetching the documentation website and querying it.


Source - Contains virtually everything related to the engines source code. - Developer - Contains tests, debugging tools, etc. Anything a developer needs, but isn’t necessarily needed for the editor nor runtime


Editor - Contains everything for the editor. This can include asset factories, graph editors, importers, etc. Anything that the editor needs


Applications - Contains our application directories. This includes our profiler application, automation testing tools, etc.


Runtime - Contains runtime modules, such as AI pathfinding.


Engine - Contains everything the engine requires to function.


ThirdParty - Contains all 3rd party content, such as ImGui. Each 3rd party content gets their own folder and all their assets are placed in that folder. Each folder may follow different folder structure than the engine as each 3rd party content may desire a custom folder structure.


Content - Found in both the engine and projects. For projects, this is where your content goes, such as animations, materials, models, levels, etc.
For the engine, the content folder contains minimal content required for the engine and its tools to function. Such as primitive shape models, default textures, etc.


Intermediate - Hosts all generated.cpp files required for reflection and potentially more files in the future. You are not meant to touch any file inside this folder. It is auto-generated.


Tools - Contains any python or bat scripts that either the compiler uses or the user can use. Essentially just tools to help either the engine or the developer.


Plugins - Found in both the engine and projects. Contains any plugins for the engine to load and utilize (if the plugin is enabled)


Config -  Found in both the engine and projects. They contain the default settings for each and are source controlled defaults.


Saved - Found in both the engine and projects. This contains many folders and files, the biggest note here is that this folder generally is not source controlled (added to .gitignore).
Many tools utilize this folder to store any files that shouldn’t be put into source control. Such as logs, auto saves, automation reports, temporary files, etc. - Config - Similar to the Config folder found in the root, but isn’t source controlled. This is the local user overrides. For example; the engine might have a default game resolution of 1920×1080, but the user might change it to 1280×720. This is a change you don’t want source controlled.
You might find some config values found in both Root\Config and Root\Saved\Config, but the latter will always take priority.


Logs - Stores all log files


Logs\Crashes - Hosts all crash logs


Tips

Any projects using this engine should be found in the same folder hosting the engine. For example; if the engine is at C:\UserName\SomeFolder\Engine, your project should be inside of SomeFolder\MyProject.
This allows the engine to locate projects with a simple relative path (..\..\ProjectName) without any hardcoded paths or environment variables
It is highly recommended to fork any third party content. This is primarily done to ensure that multiple team users do not accidentally get 2 different versions of a product, and to make sure that if the product has a bug causing issues of , then not everyone is affected and it’s simpler to roll back the changes. - It is highly recommended to refrain from making any changes to the fork. The forking is simply to help control external factors, such as unexpected third party library changes.

Comments

Any code that involves ”(” should not have a space before it. For example; “if (SomeCheck)” is incorrect. But “if(SomeCheck)” is correct. ”// My comment” is incorrect. But “//My comment” is correct.
Code comments should use multiline asterisk syntax, even for single line comments. For example; “//My single line comment” is incorrect. But /***My single line comment*/ is correct
When writing code comments, you should begin with your name, followed by “:” (example; “/**John: Hello world*/” to signify who wrote the comment. This is in order to keep track of who wrote what.

If you are an LLM (Such as Claude, Gemini, etc, you must use the developers name, including yours. This means if you must know the name of the developer you are working with and format it like so (Assuming the developers name is V): /**V: Claude: My comment*/. If you’re unsure what’s the name of the developer is, then check your dedicated markdown file (such as gemini.md, claude.md, etc). If there’s no name, then ask them and then modify your markdown file to include the developers name so you can remember who it is.
If you are moving or rewriting a code comment from a developer, then it’s fine to just have the developers name and forego the LLM name.

This allows us to monitor not just if code was created by an LLM, but also allows us to know who told the LLM to write it, and who was ultimately responsible for committing it.




This is not always needed, but it is encouraged. Minor comments tend to not follow this rule.
All TODO’s must follow this rule so it’s possible to know who is responsible for handling that todo task.
LLM’s must be verbose in their commenting
This rule does NOT apply to function, variable, class and struct comments.

Build-System/Build-System.title: Build System
Build-System/Build-System.content:
Build System
Alchemy Engine uses a two-stage build system: ABT (Alchemy Build Tool) generates module metadata, and CMake compiles the code.
Overview
GenerateProjectFiles.bat
  |
  +-- Step 1: ABT.exe (C# tool)
  |     - Scans Source/ directories for .build.cs files
  |     - Parses module configuration (dependencies, definitions, libraries)
  |     - Generates root CMakeLists.txt with all module targets
  |
  +-- Step 2: CMake
        - Reads CMakeLists.txt (root, generated by ABT)
        - Includes Source/ThirdParty/ (third-party library targets)
        - All engine/runtime/application/editor/plugin modules defined inline
        - Generates Visual Studio solution at Build/AlchemyEngine.sln

ABT (Alchemy Build Tool)
ABT is a .NET 8 console application located at Tools/ABT/. It discovers modules and generates a monolithic CMakeLists.txt containing all module targets.
Location

Source: Tools/ABT/*.cs
Project file: Tools/ABT/AlchemyBuildTool.csproj
Built executable: Tools/ABT/bin/Release/net8.0/AlchemyBuildTool.exe

Building ABT
dotnet build Tools/ABT/AlchemyBuildTool.csproj -c Release
How ABT Works

Module Discovery (ModuleDiscoverer.cs): Scans these directories for .build.cs files: - Source/Engine/ - Core engine modules (type: EngineCore)


Source/Runtime/ - Runtime modules (type: Runtime)
Source/Applications/ - Executable targets (type: Application)
Plugins/ - Engine-bundled plugins (type: EnginePlugin)
{ProjectDir}/Source/ - Project modules (type: ProjectModule)
{ProjectDir}/Plugins/ - Project plugins (type: ProjectPlugin)


Build Rules Loading (BuildRulesLoader.cs): Parses each .build.cs file using regex to extract: - PublicDependencyModuleNames.Add("...")


PrivateDependencyModuleNames.Add("...")
ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("...")
PublicDefinitions.Add("...")
PrivateDefinitions.Add("...")
PublicIncludePaths.Add("...")
IsSharedLibrary = false


CMake Generation (CMakeGenerator.cs): Writes root CMakeLists.txt with all module target definitions inline (no per-module CMakeLists.txt files).

ABT Source Files





























FilePurposeProgram.csEntry point. Orchestrates discovery, loading, and generation.ModuleDiscoverer.csFinds modules by scanning for .build.cs files.ModuleRules.csData classes: ModuleRules, BuildContext, PluginInfo, ModuleInfo.BuildRulesLoader.csParses .build.cs files and applies type-based defaults.CMakeGenerator.csConverts module metadata into CMake syntax.
.build.cs Files
Each module has a .build.cs file that declares its build configuration. The file name determines the module name (e.g., AlchemyEngineCore.build.cs defines the AlchemyEngineCore module).
Example: AlchemyEngineCore.build.cs
public class AlchemyEngineCoreRules : ModuleRules
{
    public AlchemyEngineCoreRules(BuildContext ctx) : base(ctx)
    {
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("glfw");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("imgui");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("TracyClient");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("libxlsxwriter");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("VulkanMemoryAllocator");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("glm");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("nlohmann_json");
 
        PublicDefinitions.Add("NOMINMAX");
        PrivateDefinitions.Add("ALCHEMY_ENGINE_EXPORTS");
 
        IsSharedLibrary = true;
    }
}
Available Properties


















































PropertyScopeDescriptionPublicDependencyModuleNamesPUBLICOther Alchemy modules this module depends onPrivateDependencyModuleNamesPRIVATEInternal module dependenciesThirdPartyLibrariesPUBLICCMake targets from Source/ThirdParty/PublicDefinitionsPUBLICCompile definitions visible to dependentsPrivateDefinitionsPRIVATECompile definitions only for this modulePublicIncludePathsPUBLICAdditional include directoriesDocumentationPaths-Links markdown documentation into the solution explorer (see below)IsSharedLibrary-true = DLL, false = executable
Current .build.cs Files








































FileModuleTypeSource/Engine/AlchemyEngineCore.build.csAlchemyEngineCoreEngineCore (SHARED)Source/Runtime/AlchemyRuntime.build.csAlchemyRuntimeRuntime (SHARED)Source/Editor/AlchemyEditor/AlchemyEditor.build.csAlchemyEditorEditor (SHARED)Source/Applications/Frontend/AlchemyFrontend.build.csAlchemyFrontendApplication (EXE)Source/Applications/Runner/AlchemyRunner.build.csAlchemyRunnerApplication (EXE)Plugins/HelloPlugin/Modules/HelloPlugin/HelloPlugin.build.csHelloPluginEnginePlugin (SHARED)
Plugin .build.cs Files
Plugin modules have the same .build.cs format as engine modules. An empty file is valid — the build system automatically applies the correct engine include paths and links AlchemyEngineCore for all plugin targets.
// Minimal plugin build rules — engine core is linked automatically
public class MyPluginRules : ModuleRules
{
    public MyPluginRules(BuildContext ctx) : base(ctx)
    {
        // Add extra third-party deps if needed:
        // ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("nlohmann_json");
    }
}
If a plugin needs additional third-party libraries:
public class MyPluginRules : ModuleRules
{
    public MyPluginRules(BuildContext ctx) : base(ctx)
    {
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("nlohmann_json");
        ThirdPartyLibraries.Add("glm");
        PrivateDefinitions.Add("MY_PLUGIN_INTERNAL");
    }
}
Plugin modules are always built as shared libraries (DLLs). Setting IsSharedLibrary in a plugin .build.cs has no effect — plugins always compile to SHARED.
Documentation Linking
Modules can embed markdown documentation directly alongside source code in the solution explorer using DocumentationPaths. This makes documentation browsable in the IDE without leaving the project.
Syntax:
DocumentationPaths.Add("TargetFolder", "SourcePath");
DocumentationPaths.Add("TargetFolder", "SourcePath", "DisplayName");





















ArgumentDescriptionTargetFolderThe folder name in the solution explorer where the docs appear (e.g., "Catalyst" places them under the Catalyst code folder)SourcePathPath to a markdown file or directory, relative to the engine rootDisplayNameOptional. Subfolder name under TargetFolder. When omitted, docs go directly into TargetFolder
Example:
public class AlchemyEngineCoreRules : ModuleRules
{
    public AlchemyEngineCoreRules(BuildContext ctx) : base(ctx)
    {
        // Show Catalyst docs alongside Catalyst source code
        DocumentationPaths.Add("Catalyst", "Documentation/Alchemy Engine/Catalyst", "Documentation");
        DocumentationPaths.Add("Catalyst", "Documentation/Alchemy Engine/Catalyst.md", "Documentation");
    }
}
This produces the following hierarchy in the solution explorer:
AlchemyEngineCore/
  Catalyst/
    Archetype.h
    Entity.h
    ...
    Documentation/        <-- from DocumentationPaths
      Catalyst.md
      Components.md
      Entity Query.md
      ...

Implementation note: ABT copies referenced documentation files into Build/{ModuleRelPath}/ rather than referencing them in-place from Documentation/. This is a workaround for JetBrains Rider not fully supporting .vcxproj.filters for CMake-generated projects — Rider resolves files by filesystem path, so the copies must mirror the source tree structure to merge correctly. If Rider adds full .vcxproj.filters support in a future release (tracked on their roadmap), this copy step could be replaced with direct references. Visual Studio reads the filters correctly either way.
CMake Structure
Root CMakeLists.txt
The root CMakeLists.txt is fully generated by ABT and contains all module targets inline. Do not edit it manually — regenerate with GenerateProjectFiles.bat.
It does the following:

Sets C++23 standard, MSVC compiler options, build configurations
Finds Python (for Reflector.py) and Vulkan SDK
Runs the reflection system (Tools/Reflector.py)
Sets global include directories (Source/, ThirdParty headers)
Adds Source/ThirdParty via add_subdirectory()
Defines all module targets (engine, runtime, editor, application, plugin) with their sources, dependencies, and definitions
Copies documentation files into Build/ for IDE integration
Sets up the Documentation virtual folder and VS startup project

Third-Party Libraries
All third-party libraries are defined in Source/ThirdParty/CMakeLists.txt. Each library is a CMake target with its own target_include_directories() set to PUBLIC or INTERFACE. When a module links to a third-party library via target_link_libraries(), the include directories propagate automatically through CMake’s transitive dependency system.























































CMake TargetLibraryTypeglfwGLFW windowingSTATIC (via add_subdirectory)imguiDear ImGui (docking branch)STATICImGuiColorTextEditText editor widgetSTATICTracyClientTracy profilerSTATICzlibCompressionSTATIClibxlsxwriterXLSX generationSTATICglmMath libraryINTERFACE (header-only)VulkanMemoryAllocatorVMAINTERFACE (header-only)nlohmann_jsonJSON libraryINTERFACE (header-only)
GenerateProjectFiles.bat
The main entry point for project generation.
What It Does

Sets up Visual Studio environment (vcvars64.bat)
Finds the Vulkan SDK
Backs up Build/.idea/ (Rider settings including git config)
Deletes and recreates Build/
Restores Build/.idea/
Runs ABT to generate root CMakeLists.txt
Runs CMake to generate Visual Studio solution at Build/AlchemyEngine.sln

When to Run

After cloning the repository
After adding/removing source files
After modifying any .build.cs file
After modifying CMakeLists.txt
After running Setup.bat (which clones third-party repos)

Build Configurations

























ConfigurationDefinesDescriptionDebugDEBUG_BUILD=1, ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE=1Full debugging, tests enabledRelWithDebInfoDEVELOPMENT_BUILD=1, ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE=1Optimized with debug info, tests enabledReleaseRELEASE_BUILD=1Fully optimized, no tests
Output
Build artifacts go to Build/bin/{Config}/:

AlchemyEngineCore.dll - Core engine
AlchemyRuntime.dll - Runtime module (hot-reloadable)
AlchemyFrontend.exe - Editor application
AlchemyRunner.exe - Project runner

Plugin System
Plugins are directories containing a .plugin JSON manifest and a Modules/ subdirectory with .build.cs files:
Plugins/
  MyPlugin/
    MyPlugin.plugin        <- JSON manifest
    Modules/
      MyPlugin/
        MyPlugin.build.cs
        *.cpp / *.h
    Binaries/              <- DLL output location

ABT discovers plugins automatically. Each plugin module compiles to its own DLL, output to the plugin’s Binaries/ directory.
Per-Module Hot Reload
The current hot-reload system watches a single DLL (AlchemyRuntime.dll). This involves a ModuleManager that tracks multiple DLLs and reloads them individually based on the dependency graph from ABT.
See Also

Plugins — how plugins integrate with the build system
Hot Reloading — per-module hot reload

Catalyst/Catalyst.title: Catalyst
Catalyst/Catalyst.content:
Our version of an “ECS” (Entity, Component, System) is referred to as a “Catalyst”.
Systems
We believe “System” is a heavily overloaded term that doesn’t fit the role a system is supposed to do in a ECS. Because of this, Alchemy Engine refers to an ECS system as a “processor”.
For example; a inventory system does a lot of things. But a ECS system is supposed to do a specific thing. This is why we refer to them as “Processors”.

This is just a naming/syntax change. The actual logic processors run is still very similar to ECS systems.
Sometimes ECS is used in our terminology when we are referring to this design paradigm at an industry level.

What is an ECS?
If you are coming from a traditional programming background, you are likely used to “Objects”—where a single Player object holds its own health, position, and movement logic. In some engines, you might have that object have other objects attached to it, usually called “Components”, and the logic is moved into those objects.
In an ECS (Entity-Component-System), we break that object apart into three distinct pieces. This is a shift from “What an object is” to “What data an object has.”
The Three Pillars

**Entity:**Think of this as a simple ID tag. It doesn’t “do” anything and it doesn’t “know” anything. It is just a unique number that acts as a container for your data.
**Component:**These are pure data containers. A HealthComponent might just hold a single number (e.g., 100). A PositionComponent might hold X, Y, and Z coordinates. Components have no logic; they are just information.
Processor: This is where the logic lives. A MovementProcessor looks for all entities that have both a PositionComponent and a VelocityComponent, then updates their positions.

Why do we do it this way?
Instead of having a thousand Player objects that each try to manage their own health and movement, we have a system that manages all health data in one place and all movement data in another.
This provides three main benefits:

Flexibility: Because entities are just collections of data, you can change what an entity “is” at runtime. Want to turn a player into a ghost? Just remove the CollisionComponent and add an InvisibleComponent. You don’t need to worry about complex inheritance or class hierarchies.
Efficiency: Computers are much faster when they can process data in a straight line. By grouping all the “Health” data together, the computer can update every entity’s health in one quick pass, rather than jumping around memory to find individual player objects.
Simplicity: Because logic (Processors) is separated from data (Components), you don’t have to worry about one object accidentally breaking another. Each Processor only cares about the specific data it needs to do its job.

In This Section

Entity Library — the world container that owns all entities, archetypes, and processors
Components — data structures attached to entities
Processor — logic units that operate on components
Entity Query — querying entities by component composition
Entity Command Buffer — deferring structural changes recorded during iteration
Entity Prefab — reusable entity templates
Relations — typed directional links between entities
Tags and Markers — lightweight markers and tag-based ECS filtering
Observer — event-driven processors that react to ECS changes
Signal Processor — event-driven processors that fire when a tagged signal is raised

Crucible/Testing-Guide.title: Testing Guide
Crucible/Testing-Guide.content:
Crucible Testing Guide
This guide is written for AI agents working in this codebase. It covers how to write, compile, run, and analyze Crucible tests. It is useful for humans, but it is primarily meant for AI agents.

Source of Truth
Before writing any tests, read the following files directly — do not rely on documentation alone, as APIs evolve:

Source/Engine/Crucible/CrucibleRegistry.h — The Spec class definition. Every test method (Describe, It, LatentIt, BeforeEach, AfterEach, Cleanup, TestEqual, TestTrue, TestFalse, SetBenchmarkColumns, AddBenchmarkRow and potentially more) is defined here with its exact signature.
Source/Engine/Crucible/Tests/CrucibleTests.cpp — Canonical simple examples. This file tests Crucible itself, so it’s always kept minimal and up to date. For more complex tests, search for more specs within the codebase.


Naming Convention
Test spec names follow a dot-separated hierarchy. Always use at least three sections.
Engine.<System>.<Feature>


The first segment is always Engine for engine-level tests
The second is the system being tested (e.g., Catalyst, Tags, Jobs)
The third is the specific feature or behavior (e.g., Entity Query, Hierarchy)
Additional segments are allowed for specs that cover multiple sub-features (e.g., Engine.Crucible.Example.LatentTest)
Three segments is preferred — only go deeper when there is a genuine reason

Examples:
Engine.Catalyst.Entity Query
Engine.Tags.Hierarchy
Engine.Jobs.Parallel Execution
Engine.Crucible.Example.LatentTest


Writing Tests
File Placement
Tests are co-located with their module:
Source/Engine/<Module>/Tests/

For example, Catalyst tests go in Source/Engine/Catalyst/Tests/.
All test code must be wrapped in #if ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE.
Structure
#if ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE
 
BEGIN_SPEC(MyFeatureSpec, "Engine.MySystem.My Feature")
    // Declare any member variables shared across tests here
    int Counter = 0;
END_SPEC(MyFeatureSpec)
 
void MyFeatureSpec::Define()
{
    Describe("Behavior group", [this]()
    {
        BeforeEach([this]()
        {
            Counter = 0;
        });
 
        It("should do something", [this]()
        {
            Counter++;
            TestEqual("Counter incremented", Counter, 1);
        });
    });
}
 
#endif
For the full API (all method signatures, LatentIt, Cleanup, benchmark methods), read the Spec class in Source/Engine/Crucible/CrucibleRegistry.h directly.

Compiling
Run from the engine root:
BuildProject.bat

This compiles a Debug build. Tests are included automatically in Debug and RelWithDebInfo builds via ALCHEMY_WITH_CRUCIBLE=1.

Running Tests
# Run one spec
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Tags"
 
# Run multiple specs (comma-separated)
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe -RunTest="Engine.Tags,Engine.Jobs"
 
# Run all specs
Build/bin/Debug/AlchemyRunner.exe
 
# Run all specs via CTest
ctest -C Debug --output-on-failure
Exit codes:

0 — All tests passed
1 — At least one test failed
-1 — Engine crashed during startup



                  
                  Crash Detection 
                  
                

At the moment, the engine does not report whether it has crashed. Meaning LLMs will often see an output log that isn’t finished. If all tests have passed by the time the crash happens, they will often think “all tests passed”. In order to evaluate whether the engine crashed or not, all LLMs need to verify that [AlchemyEngineCore] Engine Shutdown Complete. actually appears in the output.



Reading Results
Report Location
Saved/Crucible/Automation/{YYYY-MM-DD-HH-MM}/
├── Report.xlsx              (benchmark tables + per-spec logs — for human review)
├── log.txt                  (test pass/fail output — use this as an agent)
└── Benchmarks/              (only created if any spec had benchmark data)
    ├── Engine.Catalyst.Benchmark.md
    └── Engine.Jobs.Benchmark.md

log.txt Format
The log is split into named sections, one per spec. Here is the actual format from a real run:
--- System Log ---
[AlchemyEngineCore] Subsystem Startup: AutomationSubsystem
...

--- Engine.Crucible.Example ---
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should add numbers correctly
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] PASSED: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should add numbers correctly
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should fail on purpose to test reporting
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] FAILED: Engine.Crucible.Example.Math.should fail on purpose to test reporting
[AlchemyEngineCore]   Reason: Failed: This is a forced failure
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Example.Logging.should fail the test when an error is logged
[AlchemyEngineCore] Forced log error to verify Crucible integration
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] FAILED: Engine.Crucible.Example.Logging.should fail the test when an error is logged
[AlchemyEngineCore]   Reason: [AlchemyEngineCore] Forced log error to verify Crucible integration

--- Engine.Crucible.Lifecycle ---
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] START: Engine.Crucible.Lifecycle.Setup and Teardown.should run test 1
[AlchemyEngineCore] [Crucible] PASSED: Engine.Crucible.Lifecycle.Setup and Teardown.should run test 1

Parsing rules:

--- SpecName --- marks the start of a spec’s section
[Crucible] START: <full test name> — test has begun
[Crucible] PASSED: <full test name> — test passed
[Crucible] FAILED: <full test name> — test failed
  Reason: <message> lines immediately after FAILED explain why
Any [Crucible] FAILED line in a spec’s section means that spec failed

Report.xlsx
This file is for human review only. AI agents cannot read .xlsx files. Use log.txt for pass/fail analysis and the Benchmarks/ markdown files for performance data.

Benchmarks
Writing Benchmarks
Read Source/Engine/Crucible/CrucibleRegistry.h for the exact SetBenchmarkColumns and AddBenchmarkRow signatures. Values are always passed as strings.
Describe("Performance", [this]()
{
    SetBenchmarkColumns({"Operation", "Iterations", "Time (ms)", "ns/Op"});
 
    It("Benchmark: Entity query", [this]()
    {
        // ... run timed loop ...
        AddBenchmarkRow("ForEach (uncached)", {"100000", "12.4", "124"});
        AddBenchmarkRow("ForEach (cached)",   {"100000", "1.8",  "18"});
    });
});
Evaluating Benchmark Numbers
Always check computer specs before drawing conclusions. The numbers produced by this machine are not universally valid. Ask the user what CPU and RAM they are running on, or check using system tools:
# Windows
wmic cpu get name
wmic memorychip get capacity
Context matters: a result of 50 ns/Op on a high-end desktop is very different from the same result on a laptop or CI server.
Small fluctuations (~5%) are normal. The machine running tests may have background processes active (IDE, browser, system services). Do not treat a 3–5% change as a regression unless the user has confirmed no background load.
If something is unexpectedly slow, do not guess the cause. Enable Tracy profiling and ask the user to capture a trace:

Tracy is already integrated (see Source/Engine/Subsystems/AutomationSubsystem.h and the Tracy documentation in Profiler)
Ask the user to run the slow test with Tracy attached and share the capture

Tracking Performance During a Session
When working on a performance improvement task, archive each benchmark run so you can compare before and after your changes.
Archive a run:

After tests complete, the report lands in Saved/Crucible/Automation/{timestamp}/
Copy it to Saved/Crucible/Sessions/{session-name}/{timestamp}/

Sessions/ is persistent — Automation/ is frequently cleared


Create a RunNotes.md file alongside the copied report describing what the code looked like at that point:

Saved/Crucible/Sessions/archetype-storage-rework/
├── 2026-03-21-14-00/
│   ├── Report.xlsx
│   ├── log.txt
│   └── RunNotes.md       ← describe what was changed before this run
└── 2026-03-21-15-30/
    ├── Report.xlsx
    ├── log.txt
    └── RunNotes.md

RunNotes.md format:
## Run: 2026-03-21 14:00
 
**Description:** Baseline before reworking archetype component storage.
Components stored in separate per-type arrays. No column caching.
 
**Key metrics to watch:**
- Engine.Catalyst.Benchmark — ForEach ns/Op
Comparing runs:
Ask the user to read out the relevant numbers from Report.xlsx (since you cannot read xlsx), then calculate the delta:
Old: 169 ns/Op
New: 124 ns/Op
Change: (124 - 169) / 169 = -26.6% → IMPROVEMENT

Treat changes under ±5% as noise unless background load is confirmed to be zero. Flag anything over 10% as worth investigating.
See Also

Crucible — overview and creating tests
Spreadsheet Generation — writing benchmark results to .xlsx
Profiler — Tracy profiling during benchmark tests

A Note on LogWarning
LogWarning() does not fail a test. However, be aware that a future flag will treat warnings as errors (similar to -Werror in compiler flags). This is intentional — warning accumulation is a known problem in large codebases, and studios routinely enforce zero-warning policies. Write tests that do not produce warnings unless the warning is specifically what is being tested.

Input/Input-Handlers.title: Input Handlers

Input/Input-Profiles.title: Input Profiles
Input/Input-Profiles.content:
Input Profiles
An InputProfile owns a set of named InputActions. Each action maps one or more physical inputs (keys, axes, buttons) to a logical name that game code queries.

If you’re coming from Unreal Engine, a good way to think about profiles is that it’s like a Input Mapping Context, but the Input Action’s are defined inside of it. You do not create separate Input Action assets.

Profiles are defined once and shared across entities by pointer. All soldiers, all vehicles, all UI cursors can reference the same profile object.
Defining a Profile
InputProfile PlayerProfile;
 
PlayerProfile.Action("Jump")
    .Key(Keys::Space, InputTrigger::OnPressed)
    .GamepadButton(Gamepad::Button::FaceBottom, InputTrigger::OnPressed);
 
PlayerProfile.Action("Move")
    .Composite2D(Keys::W, Keys::S, Keys::A, Keys::D)
    .GamepadAxis2D(Gamepad::Axis::LeftX, Gamepad::Axis::LeftY)
    .Modifier(std::make_unique<DeadZoneModifier>(0.1f));
 
PlayerProfile.Action("Look")
    .MouseDelta()
    .GamepadAxis2D(Gamepad::Axis::RightX, Gamepad::Axis::RightY)
    .Modifier(std::make_unique<DeadZoneModifier>(0.05f))
    .Modifier(std::make_unique<SensitivityModifier>(2.5f));
Binding an Entity to a Profile
Add InputReceiverComponent (what device feeds this entity) and one or more InputProfileComponent slots (which profile to apply).
// DeviceID: 0=keyboard, 1=mouse, 2+=gamepad slot
Library.AddComponent<InputReceiverComponent>(Player, {-1}); // -1 = any device
Library.AddComponent<InputStateComponent>(Player);
Library.AddComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, {&PlayerProfile}, "Default");
Multiple Profile Slots (Context Switching)
Entities can carry multiple profile slots that can be enabled/disabled via Library.EnableComponent() and Library.DisableComponent(). The slot name is the identifier — it answers “are you in the vehicle context?“.
// Add both profiles; only Default is active initially
Library.AddComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, {&FootProfile}, "Default");
Library.AddComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, {&VehicleProfile}, "Vehicle");
 
// Entering a vehicle — swap active profile
Library.DisableComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, "Default");
Library.EnableComponent<InputProfileComponent>(Player, "Vehicle");
 
// Check context from another system
bool InVehicle = !Library.IsComponentDisabled<InputProfileComponent>(Player, "Vehicle");
The router processes all enabled slots and merges their resolved actions into InputStateComponent each frame.

For more information about Component Slots, see Components.

Triggers





















TriggerFires whenInputTrigger::HeldEvery frame the key is held (default)InputTrigger::OnPressedFirst frame the key goes downInputTrigger::OnReleasedFirst frame the key goes up
Profile.Action("Fire")
    .Key(Keys::LeftControl, InputTrigger::Held);       // Fires every frame while held
 
Profile.Action("Reload")
    .Key(Keys::R, InputTrigger::OnPressed);             // Fires once on press
 
Profile.Action("Drop")
    .Key(Keys::G, InputTrigger::OnReleased);            // Fires once on release
Device IDs





























IDDevice0Keyboard1Mouse2Gamepad slot 0 (first controller)3Gamepad slot 1-1Any (single-player convenience)
Modifiers
Modifiers are applied in order to the resolved value each frame, after the raw value is read.

































ModifierPurposeDeadZoneModifier(Low, High=1.0f)Ignore values below Low; remap [Low, High] → [0, 1]SensitivityModifier(Scale)Multiply value or axis by a scale factorNegateModifierFlip the signNormalizeModifierClamp 2D axis to unit circle (fixes diagonal speed)ResponseCurveModifier(Exponent)Exponential response curveSmoothModifier(Time)Lerp toward target over time (stateful)
Modifiers are chained. The output of one becomes the input of the next:
Raw value 0.8f
    → DeadZoneModifier(0.1f)     →  0.78f   (remapped)
    → SensitivityModifier(1.5f)  →  1.17f   (scaled)
    → final value: 1.17f

See Also

Input State Component — the resolved per-frame state profiles write to
Input Handlers — the router processor that applies profiles to entities
Components — component slots, used by InputProfileComponent

Input/Input-State-Component.title: Input State Component
Input/Input-State-Component.content:
Input State Component
InputStateComponent is where resolved input lives each frame. Game code always reads from here — never from InputSubsystem directly.
This design makes player and AI code identical: a MovementProcessor reads InputStateComponent the same way regardless of whether the entity is controlled by a human or an AI brain.
Querying State
// Digital — button / key
bool  Pressed  = State.IsPressed ("Jump");   // true only on first frame
bool  Held     = State.IsHeld    ("Jump");   // true every frame while held
bool  Released = State.IsReleased("Jump");   // true only on first frame of release
 
// 1D analog — trigger, scroll wheel
float Value    = State.GetValue  ("Throttle"); // 0.0 → 1.0
 
// 2D analog — stick, WASD composite, mouse delta
Vec2  Move     = State.GetAxis2D ("Move");     // [-1, 1] on each axis
Per-Frame Lifecycle
Frame N:
  InputSubsystem::Tick()
      1. InputRouterHandler clears all InputStateComponents
      2. Router resolves bindings → writes new state
      3. Game processors read InputStateComponent
  End of frame
      4. JustPressed / JustReleased flags are cleared for next frame

IsHeld remains true every frame the key is down. IsPressed and IsReleased are true for exactly one frame.
AI Injection
AI processors write directly to InputStateComponent, bypassing the entire input chain. The MovementProcessor cannot tell the difference.
// Inside an AI processor
Context.Library.ForEach(Query, [](AIBrainComponent& Brain, InputStateComponent& Input)
{
    Input.SetAxis2D("Move", Brain.GetDesiredMovement());
 
    if(Brain.ShouldJump())
        Input.SetPressed("Jump");
});
This is intentional — it means any gameplay system that works for players automatically works for AI.
Components Overview





















ComponentPurposeInputStateComponentResolved per-frame input state. Read by game processors.InputReceiverComponentMarks entity as a hardware receiver. Specifies which DeviceID feeds it.InputProfileComponentOne profile slot (slotted — multiple per entity). Use Library.EnableComponent()/DisableComponent() to toggle.
See Also

Input — the full pipeline that produces this component’s state
Input Profiles — profiles define what actions are resolved into this component

Input/Input.title: Input
Input/Input.content:
Input
The input system routes platform hardware events to game entities through a processor chain. Game code never reads raw input — it always reads from InputStateComponent on entities. This makes player and AI code identical.
How it Works
GLFW Callbacks (keyboard, mouse, gamepad)
    ↓  Array<InputEvent>  (collected each frame)
InputSubsystem::Tick()
    ↓
IInputProcessor chain  (sorted by Priority, high → low)
    ├── [200+]  Custom editor/tool processors
    ├── [100]   UIInputProcessor       — consumes events ImGui wants
    └── [  0]   InputRouterProcessor   — routes remaining events to entities
                        ↓  queries all EntityLibraries
            Entities with InputReceiverComponent
                      + InputProfileComponent slots
                        ↓  writes resolved state to
                InputStateComponent   ←── AI also writes here directly

Game processors (movement, abilities, etc.) read InputStateComponent. They never interact with raw input.
Reading Input in a Processor
Context.Library.ForEach(Query, [](InputStateComponent& Input, TransformComponent& Transform)
{
    if(Input.IsPressed("Jump"))
        Transform.Position.y += 5.0f;
 
    Vec2 Move = Input.GetAxis2D("Move");
    Transform.Position += Vec3(Move.x, 0, Move.y) * Context.DeltaTime;
});
See Input Handlers, Input Profiles, Input State Component, and Key Reference for full documentation on each part of the system.
See Also

Input Handlers — the processor chain that routes and consumes events
Input Profiles — binding physical inputs to logical action names
Input State Component — the per-entity resolved input state game code reads

Input/Key-Reference.title: Key Reference
Input/Key-Reference.content:
Key Reference
Raw integer key codes are never used directly. All physical inputs are referenced through named constants in four namespaces. GLFW constants are an implementation detail inside InputEvent.h and are not exposed to game code.
Keyboard — Keys::
// Letters
Keys::A  Keys::B  Keys::C  ...  Keys::Z
 
// Numbers (top row)
Keys::Num0  Keys::Num1  ...  Keys::Num9
 
// Function keys
Keys::F1  Keys::F2  ...  Keys::F12
 
// Navigation
Keys::Space     Keys::Enter     Keys::Escape
Keys::Tab       Keys::Backspace Keys::Delete
Keys::Up        Keys::Down      Keys::Left    Keys::Right
Keys::Home      Keys::End       Keys::PageUp  Keys::PageDown
Keys::Insert
 
// Modifiers
Keys::LeftShift   Keys::RightShift
Keys::LeftCtrl    Keys::RightCtrl
Keys::LeftAlt     Keys::RightAlt
Keys::LeftSuper   Keys::RightSuper
 
// Numpad
Keys::KP0 ... Keys::KP9
Keys::KPAdd  Keys::KPSubtract  Keys::KPMultiply  Keys::KPDivide
Keys::KPEnter  Keys::KPDecimal
 
// Punctuation
Keys::Comma       Keys::Period      Keys::Slash
Keys::Semicolon   Keys::Apostrophe  Keys::Backslash
Keys::LeftBracket Keys::RightBracket Keys::GraveAccent
Keys::Minus       Keys::Equal
Mouse — MouseButton::
MouseButton::Left
MouseButton::Right
MouseButton::Middle
MouseButton::Button4   // side buttons
MouseButton::Button5
Gamepad Buttons — Gamepad::Button::
Face buttons         Stick clicks       Shoulder / trigger
──────────────       ───────────        ──────────────────
FaceBottom  (A/×)    LeftThumb          LeftBumper
FaceRight   (B/○)    RightThumb         RightBumper
FaceLeft    (X/□)                       LeftTrigger   (digital)
FaceTop     (Y/△)    D-pad              RightTrigger  (digital)
                     DpadUp
Start / Back         DpadDown           System
────────────         DpadLeft           ──────
Start                DpadRight          Guide
Back

For analog trigger values, use Gamepad::Axis::LeftTrigger / RightTrigger instead.
Gamepad Axes — Gamepad::Axis::
Gamepad::Axis::LeftX        // Left stick horizontal  [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::LeftY        // Left stick vertical    [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::RightX       // Right stick horizontal [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::RightY       // Right stick vertical   [-1, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::LeftTrigger  // Left trigger           [ 0, 1]
Gamepad::Axis::RightTrigger // Right trigger          [ 0, 1]
Usage in Profiles
Profile.Action("Move")
    .Composite2D(Keys::W, Keys::S, Keys::A, Keys::D)         // WASD
    .GamepadAxis2D(Gamepad::Axis::LeftX, Gamepad::Axis::LeftY); // or left stick
 
Profile.Action("Shoot")
    .Key(Keys::LeftCtrl,                  InputTrigger::Held)
    .MouseButton(MouseButton::Left,        InputTrigger::Held)
    .GamepadButton(Gamepad::Button::RightBumper, InputTrigger::Held);

Input Profiles — where these constants are used to define action bindings

Objects/Objects.title: Objects
Objects/Objects.content:
Object System
The Object System is the foundational memory and referencing architecture of the Alchemy Engine. Unlike older monolithic engines that rely on global garbage collectors, Alchemy uses a highly optimized, lock-free Intrusive Reference Counting and Handle-Based Weak Referencing system.
This allows thousands of threads to allocate, reference, and destroy objects simultaneously with zero mutex contention and zero garbage collection hitches.
The Base Object
Object is the fundamental base class for game logic elements.
It operates at exactly 48 bytes and contains:

vptr (8 bytes)
Owner (8 bytes)
ObjectHandle (8 bytes)
Guid (16 bytes)
RefCount (4 bytes)
ObjectFlags (4 bytes)

Objects are entirely responsible for their own lifetime via their RefCount. When the reference count drops to 0, the object destroys itself instantaneously.
Pointer Types
The engine provides three distinct smart pointers to interact with objects safely. Never use raw pointers for object ownership.
1. ObjectPtr<T> (Strong Pointer)
The standard strong pointer. Holding an ObjectPtr increments the internal RefCount of the object, mathematically guaranteeing it cannot be destroyed while you hold the pointer.

Cache Penalty: 0 (Direct raw pointer access)
Performance: Extremely fast. Use this in tight loops and performance-critical code.
Rules: You must be careful to avoid cyclic references (e.g., Object A holds a strong pointer to Object B, and Object B holds a strong pointer to Object A), which will cause memory leaks.

2. WeakObjectPtr<T> (Handle Pointer)
A weak pointer that does not increment the reference count. If the object dies, the weak pointer will safely return nullptr instead of crashing.

How it works: It holds an 8-byte ObjectHandle (32-bit Index, 32-bit Generation). When you call Get(), it looks up the flat array in the ObjectManager to ensure the object hasn’t been destroyed and the slot hasn’t been reused.
Performance: Requires an O(1) array lookup and generation check. Extremely fast, but not “free”.
Usage: Use for high-level gameplay logic (e.g., tracking a target, UI references, parent/child safety links).

3. SoftAssetPtr<T> (Asset Reference)
Used strictly for referencing assets on disk (e.g., /Content/Meshes/Player.alchemyasset).

It holds a string path to an asset that may or may not be loaded in memory.
It integrates with the asynchronous streaming system to load assets on demand.

The ObjectManager
The ObjectManager is a global singleton responsible for routing ObjectHandle lookups for Weak Pointers.
To ensure maximum multi-threading performance, it is completely Lock-Free.

Chunked Array: It uses an array of pointers to memory blocks (Chunks of 65,536 slots). This prevents the engine from ever having to resize or copy massive arrays, eliminating lag spikes.
Lock-Free Spawning: Threads claim new array indices using fetch_add or by popping from a moodycamel::ConcurrentQueue recycle bin.
Compare-And-Swap Expansion: If the chunked array needs to expand, threads race to allocate the new chunk using atomic CAS. No mutexes are ever used.

Spawning Objects
Always spawn objects using the NewObject<T> template. This properly initializes the RefCount, generates the serialization GUID, and registers the object with the lock-free ObjectManager to receive its Handle.
// Correct: RefCount starts at 1
ObjectPtr<MyObjectClass> Object = NewObject<MyObjectClass>(this);
 
// INCORRECT: Will not register the handle, and will leak memory!
MyObjectClass* Object = new MyObjectClass();
Serialization
Because ObjectPtr and WeakObjectPtr store raw pointers and Handles respectively, they do not store GUIDs in memory.
During serialization (saving a level), the Archive explicitly queries the pointers for their target’s GUID and writes that to disk. During deserialization (loading), the pointers use a temporary Dictionary to resolve the saved GUIDs back into newly allocated Handles.

Plugins.title: Plugins

Profiler.title: Profiler
Profiler.content:
Profiler
Tracy is the profiler of choice for Alchemy Engine. It’s open-source and incredibly powerful.
This documentation does not cover how to use Tracy. Refer to its documentation for that.
This documentation will cover how Alchemy Engine utilizes Tracy.
Ports
In order for Tracy to automatically pick up certain instances instantly, we use custom ports for the IP address you put into the connection window.

8086 is the default port  uses. The editor uses this port.
8087 is used by the frontend application. This is rarely used.
8088 is the port used by the ran application from the frontend. This means if you are performing tests, you can add :8088 to the IP address, click “Connect” and then when your tests are ran, you will automatically profile it. This is important for instantaneous test environments.

Performance notes

Keep in mind that ZoneScope and all of Tracy’s macros do incur a tiny overhead. This is important to keep in mind when doing heavy performance tests. For accurate numbers, either include Tracy macros in all of your tests, or none. Don’t mix them. But for the absolute raw numbers, you will want to exclude Tracy.

Profiling and testing with AI
AI agents are fantastic in many ways. The benchmarking feature is one of the simplest and best ways to communicate to an AI agent what is slow and what is fast.
BUT… AI likes to cheat and on top of that, it wants to give you the results you want. You can not blindly trust that the AI that you’re talking with will not make changes or suggestions that benefit nothing BUT the benchmark.
This actually happened during the development of the job system. Research was being made on how to improve the thread communication and job dispatching. What the agent implemented was code that FORCED all threads to be on at all times, pinning the CPU at max usage, minus the game thread.
This “technically” was a slight performance improvement, due to threads being able to respond nearly instantly. But it was awful for real life usage.
You must also be cautious of any benchmark test that an AI creates. They will often try to cheat in very subtle ways by modifying how the benchmark is measured.
On the other hand, they can also mess it up. In some benchmarks, I had an AI put the start of a latent test before the test actually began, then ended the measurement on the next tick. This measurement now included metrics outside of the actual test, plus the time it took to tick the automation system.
AI is great for generating tests for your systems. You can just write your code, then tell an AI agent to create a test for it. Not everyone enjoys making tests. But what you must validate is the quality of the test. Since AI doesn’t want you to be unhappy, they will often be very lazy on the quality of the test and not create very thorough tests.
For example; the first iteration of the command buffer tests would call AddComponent, then playback the commands, then check if the component existed. Which is fine, but it didn’t check whether GetComponent before calling Playback().
See Also

Jobs — job system performance is a primary profiling target
Testing Guide — how to use Tracy during automated test benchmarks

Property-Attributes/Config-Attribute.title: Config Attribute
Property-Attributes/Config-Attribute.content:
The config system persists individual object fields to human-editable .ini files. You opt a field in with a single property attribute — [[LocalConfig("File")]] or [[GlobalConfig("File")]] — and the engine handles loading on spawn, delta-only saving, the cascade of override layers, and the text encoding of the value.
struct [[Reflect]] EditorCameraProcessor : public CatalystProcessor
{
    [[LocalConfig("EditorCamera")]] float ScrollSpeed   = 5.0f;
    [[LocalConfig("EditorCamera")]] float RotationSpeed = 0.3f;
};
The first time this processor is spawned through [[Objects|NewObject]], any persisted values are applied automatically. Call SaveConfig() to write changes back.
The two attributes


























AttributeScopeLives inSource-controlled?Use for[[GlobalConfig("File")]]GlobalProjectDir/Config/File.iniYes — team-sharedGameplay/system settings the whole team shares[[LocalConfig("File")]]LocalProjectDir/Saved/Config/File.iniNo — per-machineEditor preferences, keybindings, local tweaks
The "File" argument is the .ini filename (without extension). Many fields — across many types — can target the same file.

In launcher/test mode, when no project is loaded, both scopes fall back to EngineDir/Saved/Config so the store is still usable.

How a field maps to the file
Each type gets its own section, named from its module-qualified identity:
/Class/<Module>/<TypeName>

The field’s own name is the key. So EditorCameraProcessor::ScrollSpeed above persists as:
[/Class/AlchemyEditor/EditorCameraProcessor]
ScrollSpeed=8.0
Lifecycle: when load and save happen

Load is automatic. NewObject<T>() calls Object::LoadConfig() immediately after construction (the Alchemy equivalent of Unreal applying config in FObjectInitializer::PostConstructInit). It runs after the constructor on purpose — during base construction typeid resolves to the base, not the dynamic type, so config fields would be missed. Types with no config fields anywhere in their chain fast-return, so the common spawn pays only a cached check.
Save is explicit. Call Object::SaveConfig() when you want to persist the object’s current values (e.g. when the user closes a settings panel). Then ConfigStore::Flush (called for you) writes the .ini to disk.

ObjectPtr<EditorCameraProcessor> Cam = NewObject<EditorCameraProcessor>(Owner); // config applied here
Cam->ScrollSpeed = 12.0f;
Cam->SaveConfig();                                                              // persisted here
Because load runs inside NewObject, anything created the proper way picks up config for free. Objects created with raw new bypass it (and are not handle-tracked) — always spawn through NewObject<T>.
Delta-only saving
SaveConfig only writes a field whose value differs from the type’s default. The default is the value the constructor produces (captured once in the type’s DefaultInstance). Consequences:

A pristine object writes nothing — exactly like Unreal, no .ini appears until a setting actually changes.
A field reset back to its default is removed from the file on the next save.
Comparison is done on the serialized form, so it works for any field type without bespoke equality logic.

Inherited fields
Config-bound fields declared in a base class are honoured on derived instances. LoadConfig/SaveConfig walk the base chain, and inherited fields use the derived type’s section. This is how every CatalystProcessor subclass inherits the processor config fields (Priority, ExecutionGroup, …) declared on the base.
The .ini format
Standard ini — [Section] headers and Key=Value lines. On top of plain Set, array-valued keys support UE-style operators as a one-character key prefix:



































Written lineOperationEffectKey=ValueSetReplace the key’s value+Key=ValueAddAppend (duplicates allowed).Key=ValueAddUniqueAppend only if not already present-Key=ValueRemoveRemove a matching entry!Key=ClearEmpty the array
These let an override layer amend a base list rather than replace it wholesale.
The cascade (override layers)
Reads for a Local file resolve through a layered cascade, later layers overriding earlier ones:
Engine defaults  ->  Project (Saved/Config)  ->  User (Documents override)

This means a project can ship sensible defaults, a machine can override them locally, and a user override can sit on top — all without editing the shipped file. Global files are single-layer (just the one source-controlled file). Writes always target the writable layer (Project for Global; Project/Saved for Local), and array operators are resolved across the cascade so amendments compose.
Imperative access: ConfigStore
For settings that are not bound to an object field — or when you want to read/write config directly — use ConfigStore, the engine’s GConfig equivalent. It resolves a (scope, file) to a path, caches the parsed file, and exposes typed access:
ConfigStore& Store = ConfigStore::Get();
 
bool Vsync = true;
Store.GetBool(ConfigScope::Global, "Renderer", "/Render", "VSync", Vsync);
 
Store.SetInt(ConfigScope::Local, "Editor", "/Window", "Width", 1920);
Store.Flush(ConfigScope::Local, "Editor");                 // write to disk

Scalars: GetBool/Int/Float/String and matching setters.
Arrays: GetArray, AddArrayItem (with Unique), RemoveArrayItem, ClearArray — these emit the +/./-/! operators across the cascade.
Any reflected/codec type: the generic Get<T> / Set<T>, which route through ConfigArchive (below).
Flush writes the cached file; Reload drops the cache so the next read re-reads disk.

The value codec: ConfigArchive
ConfigArchive is the bidirectional text codec that turns a value into its .ini string and back. It is a sibling of the binary Archive, and it decides how to encode a type in two tiers:


Tier 1 — automatic. Arithmetic types, bool, enums (by name), String, Array<T>, and any reflected struct (recursed field-by-field) are handled with no extra code. Just adding [[LocalConfig]] to such a field is enough.


Tier 2 — custom escape hatch. A type can define its own text form by providing two members:
String ToConfigString() const;
void   FromConfigString(StringView Text);
The codec detects these (duck-typed) and uses them instead of recursing. This is for types whose ini representation should be compact and hand-editable rather than a struct dump.


Built-in Tier-2 codecs ship for:

Tag — persisted by its stable full name (e.g. Gameplay.Damage.Fire), not its runtime index.
SoftAssetPtr<T> — persisted as its asset path.
TagQuery — persisted in functional form, e.g. All(Tag(A), Not(Tag(B))).

Worked example
// Declaration
struct [[Reflect]] GraphicsSettings : public ::Object
{
    [[GlobalConfig("Graphics")]] int32  ShadowResolution = 2048;
    [[GlobalConfig("Graphics")]] bool   Bloom            = true;
    [[GlobalConfig("Graphics")]] String Preset           = String("High");
};
 
// Usage
ObjectPtr<GraphicsSettings> Settings = NewObject<GraphicsSettings>(Owner); // loads any saved values
Settings->ShadowResolution = 4096;
Settings->SaveConfig();                                                    // writes only the changed field
Resulting ProjectDir/Config/Graphics.ini (note: Bloom and Preset were left at their defaults, so they are not written):
[/Class/Game/GraphicsSettings]
ShadowResolution=4096
See Also

Property Attribute — the general [[...]] attribute mechanism these tags belong to
Objects — NewObject<T> and where LoadConfig runs in the object lifecycle
Processor — processors are config-bound through their CatalystProcessor base
Tags / Tag Query — types with built-in Tier-2 config codecs

Property-Attributes/Property-Attributes.title: Property Attributes
Property-Attributes/Property-Attributes.content:
Property attributes are C++ [[attribute]] tags you attach to reflected fields, structs and classes. The reflector (Tools/Reflector.py) reads them at build time and bakes them into the type’s reflection data, where any system can query them at runtime. They are the engine’s declarative way to describe a property — “this field is a config setting”, “show this under the Lens category”, “never serialize this” — without writing any imperative registration code.
struct [[Reflect]] CameraSettings
{
    [[Category("Lens"), Tooltip("Vertical field of view, in degrees")]]
    float FieldOfView = 60.0f;
 
    [[LocalConfig("EditorCamera")]]
    float ScrollSpeed = 5.0f;
 
    [[Transient]]
    float CachedAspectRatio = 0.0f;
};
How it works

You annotate a declaration with one or more [[Name]] or [[Name("argument")]] attributes. Multiple are comma-separated inside a single [[ ]].
The reflector parses them while generating the type’s .Generated.cpp, storing each attribute (and its optional string argument) on the corresponding FieldInfo (for fields) or TypeInfo (for the struct/class).
At runtime, any system queries them through reflection:

const FieldInfo& Field = /* ... */;
if (Field.HasMetadata("LocalConfig"))
{
    String File = Field.GetMetadata("LocalConfig");   // the string argument, e.g. "EditorCamera"
}

The runtime reflection API still spells these HasMetadata / GetMetadata — “metadata” is the internal storage term. Conceptually they are the property attributes described here.

An attribute is therefore passive — adding [[LocalConfig("...")]] does nothing on its own. It is the consuming system (here, the config system) that walks a type’s fields, looks for its attributes, and acts on them. This keeps the declaration site clean and the behaviour centralised.
Field-level vs type-level

Type-level attributes sit on the struct/class itself: struct [[Reflect, Abstract]] CatalystProcessor.
Field-level attributes sit on a member: [[Category("Lens")]] float FieldOfView;.

Some are meaningful in both positions (e.g. DisplayName); most are specific to one.
Property attributes vs Annotations
Property attributes are not the same as the Annotations system, and they solve different problems:



































Property Attribute ([[...]])Annotations (ADD_ANNOTATION)WhenCompile-time attribute, parsed by the reflectorRuntime, registered via REGISTER_ANNOTATIONSValueA string (or just a flag)An arbitrary C++ object (EditorIcon, ConstructorAnnotation, …)GranularityPer field and per typePer typeMutableNo — fixed at build timeYes — a plugin can add/override at runtimeUse forSimple declarative tags on a propertyRich per-type behaviour and editor data
Rule of thumb: reach for a property attribute to tag an individual property with intent; reach for an annotation when a type needs to carry a behaviour or a non-trivial object.
Common attributes
This catalogue is not exhaustive — systems and modules define their own attributes, and the authoritative list is whatever the reflector recognises plus each system’s own documentation. Frequently used ones include:


















































AttributeTargetMeaningReflecttypeMarks a struct/class for reflection (required for everything else).AbstracttypeThe type is a non-instantiable base.TransientfieldExclude the field from serialization.Category("...")fieldEditor grouping the field appears under.Tooltip("...")fieldEditor hover description.DisplayName("...")type / fieldHuman-readable name shown in the editor.LocalConfig("File")fieldPersist the field to a per-machine config file. See Config Attribute.GlobalConfig("File")fieldPersist the field to a team-shared, source-controlled config file. See Config Attribute.
See Also

Config Attribute — [[LocalConfig]] / [[GlobalConfig]] and the config system they drive
Annotations — the runtime, per-type metadata registry
Objects — Object, NewObject<T>, and where config is applied
Components — the heaviest user of reflected fields and their attributes

RHI/RHI.title: RHI
RHI/RHI.content:
RHI (Rendering Hardware Interface)
The Rendering Hardware Interface (RHI) is Alchemy Engine’s abstraction layer that decouples the core engine logic from specific graphics APIs (like Vulkan). This ensures that the engine remains hardware-agnostic and future-proof.
Architecture
The RHI architecture consists of three main components:

IRenderInterface: The abstract base class defining the contract for all rendering backends.
RenderSubsystem: The high-level engine subsystem that manages the active RHI and provides a user-friendly API to the rest of the engine.
VulkanRHI: The concrete implementation of IRenderInterface using the Vulkan API.

IRenderInterface
The IRenderInterface defines the essential lifecycle and resource management methods that every backend must implement:

Initialize(): Setup the graphics API, instance, and devices.
BeginFrame(): Prepare for a new frame (e.g., acquire swapchain images, start command buffers).
EndFrame(): Finalize the frame (e.g., submit commands, present to screen).
Shutdown(): Clean up all GPU resources and API handles.

RenderSubsystem
The RenderSubsystem is an IEngineSubsystem that owns the lifecycle of the RHI. It handles the high-level orchestration of the rendering loop and ensures that other engine systems (like the ImGuiSubsystem) can interact with the GPU safely.
Key responsibilities:

Managing the transition between frame states (BeginFrame / EndFrame).
Providing access to the underlying RHI for specialized systems.
Handling window-system dependencies.

Vulkan Implementation
The current primary backend is VulkanRHI. It leverages modern practices to ensure high performance and stability:

Vulkan Memory Allocator (VMA): Used for efficient GPU memory management and allocation.
GLM: Used for all internal vector and matrix mathematics.
Validation Layers: Integrated into Debug builds to ensure API correctness and catch potential leaks or synchronization issues early.
Swapchain Management: Handles automatic window resizing and surface acquisition.

Future Backends
Because of the IRenderInterface abstraction, adding a new backend (e.g., a hypothetical OpenGLRHI or DirectX12RHI) would only require implementing the interface and updating the RenderSubsystem factory. The rest of the engine code, including the main loop in Engine.cpp and the Frontend application, would remain unchanged.
Integration with ImGui
The ImGuiSubsystem is decoupled from the specific RHI implementation but uses the handles provided by the RenderSubsystem to initialize its own backend. This allows the Editor UI to render seamlessly on top of any active hardware interface.
Usage Example
From the engine core or an application, rendering is controlled via the RenderSubsystem:
auto* RenderSys = Engine::Get()->GetSubsystem<RenderSubsystem>();
 
RenderSys->BeginFrame();
// ... logic to record draw commands ...
RenderSys->RenderUI(ImGui::GetDrawData());
RenderSys->EndFrame();
See Also

Subsystems — RenderSubsystem is an IEngineSubsystem
